meta.title: UK Politics Fact-Check League
meta.description: A daily fact-check of the most-discussed claim in UK politics, scored on a 0-100 truth meter, with a running league table of everyone featured.
meta.note: Every figure below is anchored to a named primary or established secondary source (linked). Where a claim's accuracy depends on the time period or a definition, that is stated explicitly. Verdicts should be re-confirmed against the linked sources before publication.

entries.topic: Is this the biggest defence spending increase since the Cold War?
entries.claim: This government will begin the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: The single biggest political story of the 24-48 hour window was the defence-funding crisis engulfing the government: Defence Secretary John Healey resigned on 11 June 2026, followed hours later by armed forces minister Al Carns, accusing the Treasury of refusing to commit the resources needed to defend the country and warning the Defence Investment Plan would take spending only to 2.68% of GDP by 2030 when 3% is needed. As Starmer reshuffled (appointing Dan Jarvis on 12 June) and defended his record in a BBC interview, his stock line — that Labour is delivering 'the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War' — became the most-scrutinised claim of the week, carried by the BBC, Bloomberg, CNN, the Globe and Mail, Forces News and the New Statesman, and directly contradicted by his own departing minister. The £13.4bn headline figure underpinning it was branded 'misleadingly large' and 'silly games with numbers' by the IFS, putting the claim at the centre of the resignation row.
entries.explanation: The 'since the Cold War' framing is defensible on one measure and misleading on another, and the headline number attached to it is contested. Measured in cash and real terms, the planned rise is genuinely large: after decades of decline, the government is lifting NATO-qualifying defence spending from about 2.3% of GDP in 2024 to a committed 2.6% by April 2027, with a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament — and the IFS broadly accepts that, as a sustained real-terms uplift, this is the biggest since the Cold War ended, because spending had been on a long downward path since the late 1980s. The misleading part is the implied scale. As a SHARE OF GDP, even 2.6% or 3% remains far below actual Cold War levels, when UK defence spending ran at roughly 4-6% of GDP through the 1980s — so 'since the end of the Cold War' quietly excludes the Cold War's own much higher spending. More damagingly, the £13.4bn-a-year figure the government uses to dramatise the increase was branded 'misleadingly large' by the IFS: it compares cash spending in 2027/28 with 2024/25 rather than with what spending would otherwise have been, and the genuine real-terms uplift from the 0.2-percentage-point rise is closer to £6bn — a figure Healey himself conceded. Above all, the boast was made in the same week Starmer's own Defence Secretary resigned saying the plan 'falls well short of what is required' and reaches only 2.68% of GDP by 2030, so the 'biggest increase' line is real in direction but oversells both its size and its adequacy.
evidence: Starmer (BBC interview, 11-12 June 2026, defending his record after John Healey's resignation): 'this government will begin the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War' — the government's standard framing first used when the 2.5%-by-2027 plan was announced in February 2025.

Full Fact / NATO: UK defence spending was about 2.3% of GDP in 2024 and 2.4% in 2025; the government has committed to 2.6% (NATO definition, including pensions and the Integrated Security Fund) by April 2027, with an ambition of 3% in the next Parliament — a genuine real-terms increase after a long post-Cold-War decline.

House of Commons Library / IFS: UK defence spending ran at roughly 4-6% of GDP through the 1980s and has fallen sharply since; today's ~2.3-2.6% remains well below actual Cold War levels, so 'biggest increase SINCE the Cold War' excludes the far higher Cold-War-era share of GDP.

IFS (Paul Johnson and Ben Zaranko): the £13.4bn headline figure is 'misleadingly large' — it compares 2027/28 cash with 2024/25 rather than the counterfactual; an extra 0.2% of GDP is about £6bn (the size of the aid-budget cut), and Johnson accused the PM of 'silly games with numbers' with 'totally inconsistent figures'.

John Healey, then Defence Secretary, conceded the real-terms increase 'would be something over £6 billion' and that 'the definition of defence numbers can be done in different ways'; he then resigned on 11 June 2026 saying the plan 'falls well short of what is required' and reaches only 2.68% of GDP by 2030 against a needed 3%.

Context: armed forces minister Al Carns resigned hours after Healey, and Dan Jarvis was appointed Defence Secretary on 12 June 2026; the Defence Investment Plan is due before the NATO summit on 7 July 2026, so the underlying plan was still unpublished when the claim was repeated.
entries.confidence: High — the historical GDP-share comparison (4-6% in the 1980s versus ~2.3-2.6% now) comes from the House of Commons Library and IFS, the current and committed percentages are confirmed by Full Fact and NATO data, and the critique of the £13.4bn figure is on the record from the IFS (Paul Johnson and Ben Zaranko) and was effectively conceded by Healey himself. The only soft spot is that 'sustained increase' is partly a forward-looking projection dependent on a Defence Investment Plan not yet published.

sources.title: Full Fact — UK defence spending: explained
House of Commons Library — UK defence spending (CBP-8175)
IFS — Response to defence spending announcement
New Statesman — Defence Secretary John Healey resigns over military spending

entries.topic: Who cut net migration — Labour or the Conservatives
entries.claim: The government has cut net migration by 80%. I'm not sure the Conservatives should be taking credit for this — it's happened on this government's watch.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; Labour candidate in the Makerfield by-election (18 June 2026)
entries.discussionNote: With the Makerfield by-election on 18 June shaping up as the most-watched contest of the month — Andy Burnham, a potential Starmer leadership challenger, against Reform's Robert Kenyon — the BBC by-election debate put immigration front and centre. Burnham's clash with Conservative candidate Michael Winstanley over who deserves credit for the falling numbers was the debate's most-quoted exchange, written up by AOL, MSN and the local press, and fact-checked head-on by Full Fact on 10-12 June ('Are the Conservatives or Labour responsible for the fall in net migration?'). It sits on top of immigration's continued status as the public's number-one issue and a week of Makerfield-related disinformation (AI 'Burnham' images, miscaptioned riot clips), making the credit-claiming over the migration fall one of the most-circulated factual disputes of the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: The headline number is roughly right but the credit-claiming is the misleading part. On the raw figure, Burnham is close: ONS provisional data show net migration fell from a peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 to 171,000 in the year ending December 2025 — an 82% fall, so '80%' is a fair rounding. The problem is the framing 'I'm not sure the Conservatives should be taking credit... it's happened on this government's watch', which implies Labour drove the reduction. The evidence points the other way. Measured from the year ending June 2024 — the last quarter before Labour took office in July 2024 — net migration fell about 74%, not 80%, so most of the headline drop predates this government. More importantly, both the ONS and the Migration Observatory attribute the fall mainly to visa rule changes the previous Conservative government introduced in early 2024 (restrictions on dependants of international students and care workers, and tighter Skilled Worker rules): the ONS says the drop 'may be partly because of immigration rule changes, which were introduced in 2024 and 2025', and the Migration Observatory says it was 'largely driven by policies first introduced by the previous government', with Labour's own May 2025 measures 'not yet fully visible' in the 2025 figures. So the 82% fall is real and did continue under Labour, but the bulk of it stems from Conservative-era policy still working through the data — making Burnham's bid to deny the Conservatives credit the weakest part of the claim. (His Conservative opponent Michael Winstanley's mirror-image claim, that the fall was due to 'policies brought in under the tail end of the last Conservative government', is closer to the expert consensus, though Full Fact notes no one can yet attribute the fall precisely.)
evidence: ONS Long-term international migration, provisional (released 21 May 2026): net migration 171,000 for the year ending December 2025, down from a revised peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 — a fall of about 82%, consistent with Burnham's rounded '80%'.

Full Fact (10-12 June 2026): net migration fell about 82% from the YE March 2023 peak to YE December 2025, but only about 74% since the year ending June 2024, just before Labour took office in July 2024 — so most of the fall predates this government.

ONS: 'The drop in total immigration in the latest year may be partly because of immigration rule changes, which were introduced in 2024 and 2025'; the single biggest driver was a 47% fall in non-EU+ work-related arrivals in 2025.

Migration Observatory: the decline 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations' and was 'largely driven by policies first introduced by the previous [Conservative] government' — chiefly the early-2024 restrictions on student and care-worker dependants; Labour's May 2025 changes were 'not yet fully visible' in the 2025 statistics.

Conservative candidate Michael Winstanley argued in the same debate that the fall was 'due to the policies that were brought in under the tail end of the last Conservative government where we put a restriction on visas' — a framing closer to the ONS/Migration Observatory assessment.

Caveat on attribution: Full Fact concludes 'we don't know for sure exactly which factors have driven the fall in net migration', and the 171,000 figure is provisional and prone to upward revision, so neither side can claim precise credit.
entries.confidence: High — the 82% headline fall and the 171,000 / 944,000 figures come directly from ONS provisional migration statistics, and the attribution caveats (74% under Labour; the fall largely driven by Conservative-era 2024 rule changes) are corroborated by Full Fact, the ONS commentary and the Migration Observatory. The only soft spot is that exact policy attribution is inherently uncertain and the latest figure is provisional.

sources.title: Full Fact — Are the Conservatives or Labour responsible for the fall in net migration?
ONS — Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025 (21 May 2026)
Migration Observatory — Net migration to the UK
AOL / PA — All the key points from the Makerfield by-election debate

entries.claim: On their watch, defence spending went down and welfare spending went up — £88 billion overall, with £33 billion alone under the shadow Chancellor, Sir Mel Stride.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: With anti-immigration riots in Belfast and the delayed Defence Investment Plan dominating the week, Starmer used PMQs on 10 June 2026 to turn Badenoch's attack on funding back onto the Conservative record, deploying the '£88 billion' welfare-rise line as his central rebuttal. The figure was clipped for Labour's official social channels ('Under the Tories, the welfare bill went up by £88 billion'), amplified by LBC, ITV News and the Spectator and New Statesman PMQs write-ups, and slotted into the running welfare-versus-defence-versus-tax argument that has run through Westminster all month — making it one of the most-circulated factual claims of the 24–48 hour window, and the headline number the government wants to define the Conservatives by.
entries.explanation: The headline number is broadly right but the framing is doing a lot of work. Total real-terms welfare/social-security spending in Great Britain did rise substantially across the Conservatives' 2010–2024 period: Resolution Foundation analysis (its 'Ratchets, retrenchment and reform' study and follow-ups) shows welfare rising as a share of the economy from about 10% of GDP in 2007–08 to 11.2% in 2024–25, with a real-terms increase of roughly £44 billion in just the last five years of that period (2019–20 to 2024–25 prices), so an £88bn cumulative real-terms rise over the full Conservative span is a defensible order of magnitude and matches the figure Labour has published. The crucial context Starmer omits is composition: the overwhelming majority of the increase was driven by the state pension under the triple lock (about £21bn of the recent rise) and by health and disability benefits (about £24bn of the recent rise) — categories largely outside day-to-day government control or actively protected by all parties — while spending on mainstream working-age, non-health benefits actually FELL in real terms over the same years, with benefit-receiving working-age households losing on average about £1,500 a year from cuts such as the benefit cap, two-child limit and the 2010s freezes. So 'welfare spending went up £88 billion on their watch' is numerically fair but rhetorically implies Tory largesse on out-of-work benefits, when the rise was concentrated in pensions and disability/incapacity support that the Conservatives were cutting elsewhere. The '£33 billion under Sir Mel Stride' sub-claim attributes a slice of that rise to Stride's tenure spanning DWP and the Treasury; it is plausible but harder to pin to a single published figure than the £88bn total.
evidence: Hansard, PMQs 10 June 2026: Starmer said that on the Conservatives' watch 'defence spending went down and welfare spending went up — £88 billion overall, with £33 billion alone under the shadow Chancellor, Sir Mel Stride', arguing they neither reformed welfare nor invested in the armed forces.

Resolution Foundation: total welfare spending rose as a share of GDP from about 10% in 2007–08 to 11.2% in 2024–25, and increased about £44bn in real terms between 2019–20 (£280bn) and 2025–26 (£324bn) alone — consistent with an ~£88bn cumulative real-terms rise across the full 2010–2024 Conservative period.

Of the recent £44bn rise, about £21bn was pensioner benefits (driven by the triple lock and an ageing population) and about £24bn was non-pensioner disability and incapacity benefits; spending on other working-age benefits fell by roughly £1bn in real terms.

Resolution Foundation: between 2010–11 and 2024–25, working-age households receiving benefits lost on average about £1,500 a year in real terms from policy changes (benefit cap, two-child limit, multi-year freezes) — i.e. mainstream working-age welfare was cut, not expanded, over the period.

Labour repeated the figure on its official channels ('Under the Tories, the welfare bill went up by £88 billion'), confirming the wording was a deliberate, central government line rather than an off-the-cuff number.

Caveat on attribution: the '£33bn under Mel Stride' element folds together periods in different roles and is not anchored to a single published primary figure, so it is less verifiable than the £88bn total.
entries.confidence: Medium-High — the direction and rough magnitude of the £88bn real-terms rise are well supported by Resolution Foundation analysis of DWP/OBR welfare data, and the composition caveats (pensions and disability driving the rise while working-age benefits were cut) are firmly documented. The precise £88bn cumulative figure rests on a specific real-terms baseline and deflator that the government has not fully published, and the '£33bn under Stride' sub-figure could not be confirmed against a single primary source, so the exact numbers carry some uncertainty even though the overall picture is sound.

sources.title: Hansard — Engagements (PMQs), House of Commons, 10 June 2026
Resolution Foundation — Is welfare spending 'out of control'?
Resolution Foundation — Ratchets, retrenchment and reform: the social security system since 2010
ITV News — Starmer refuses to rule out tax hikes to fund defence spending

entries.claim: Military chiefs have said the Defence Investment Plan would need £28 billion to be fully funded — will that requirement be met in full?
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: Defence dominated PMQs on 10 June 2026, with Badenoch devoting all six of her questions to the delayed Defence Investment Plan (DIP) and pressing Starmer on a reported £28bn funding gap and whether he would rule out tax rises to close it. Starmer declined to confirm the figure or rule out tax rises, saying only the plan would come 'before the NATO summit'. The clash — framed around Badenoch's charge of 'total paralysis' and 'dithering' — led Spectator, GB News, Yorkshire Post and other coverage, and sat on top of a week of warnings from Chief of the Defence Staff Sir Richard Knighton that Britain is 'running out of time' to rearm, making the £28bn figure the most-scrutinised number in UK politics this week.
entries.explanation: Badenoch is accurately repeating a figure that has been widely reported, but she presents a briefed, unpublished number as a settled official requirement, which it is not. Multiple outlets (Defense News, US News, Reuters via Daily Maverick, and the GB News/Spectator and Yorkshire Post PMQs coverage) report that 'military chiefs have warned Starmer there is a £28 billion shortfall in funding over the next four years', and that this gap is the reason the DIP has been repeatedly delayed since last year. So the £28bn figure is genuine in the sense that it is the number circulating in credible reporting and attributed to defence chiefs. Two important caveats. First, it is a media-reported figure attributed to unnamed 'military chiefs', not a costing published by the MoD, NAO or OBR — the DIP itself was still unpublished when Badenoch spoke, so 'fully funded' is being measured against a plan the public cannot yet see. Second, the framing implies a fixed, agreed requirement, whereas it represents the gap between what chiefs say is needed for 'warfighting readiness' and the funding currently pencilled in, within an envelope the government says rises to 2.6% of GDP by April 2027 (from 2.4% in 2025) and a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament. The number is therefore accurately quoted but rests on briefing rather than a primary published source, and its status as a hard 'requirement' is contested.
evidence: Defense News (5 June 2026): 'Media reports say military chiefs have warned Starmer that there is a £28 billion ($38 billion) shortfall in funding over the next four years', cited as the reason for the DIP delay.

The figure is corroborated across US News, Daily Maverick (Reuters), Global Banking & Finance and the GB News/Spectator and Yorkshire Post PMQs write-ups — but none attributes it to a named official or a published MoD/NAO/OBR document; it is consistently described as a media-reported, briefed figure.

Spectator PMQs coverage (10 June 2026): Badenoch 'pointed out that military chiefs had said the DIP would need £28 billion to be fully funded' and asked 'will that requirement be met in full?'; Starmer replied ministers were 'working through the details'.

The DIP, intended to fund the move to 'warfighting readiness', was originally due last autumn and remained unpublished; Defence Secretary John Healey said it would be published before the NATO summit on 7 July 2026.

Context on the funding envelope: Full Fact and the House of Commons Library record UK defence spending at about 2.4% of GDP in 2025, rising to a committed 2.6% by April 2027 (Starmer: '£270 billion over the Parliament'), with a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament and 3.5% by 2035 under the new NATO target.

Chief of the Defence Staff Sir Richard Knighton warned on 5 June 2026 that the UK faces its most dangerous security environment since the Cold War and is 'running out of time' to modernise, lending weight to the chiefs' funding warnings.
entries.confidence: Medium — the £28bn figure is consistently reported across several independent outlets and attributed to military chiefs, and Badenoch quotes it accurately, but it originates in briefing rather than a published primary costing (MoD/NAO/OBR), so the precise figure and its status as a fixed 'requirement' cannot be fully verified against a primary source. The surrounding GDP figures are confirmed by Full Fact and the Commons Library.

sources.title: Defense News — UK running out of time to boost defense as investment plan stalls, military chief warns
The Spectator — Keir Starmer's weakness was on display at PMQs
Full Fact — Is the UK's defence spending increasing?
House of Commons Library — UK defence spending

entries.claim: Latest stats show we're over 70% of the way to our target to recruit 6,500 teachers in the places they're needed most — 4,654 more teachers in those schools and colleges compared to 2024.
entries.speaker: Bridget Phillipson
entries.discussionNote: The DfE's annual school workforce statistics, published 4 June 2026, showed the total teaching workforce shrinking for the first time since 2019 even as ministers claimed progress on the flagship '6,500 new teachers' pledge. Phillipson's '70% of the way' framing was picked up by Schools Week, TES, Channel 4 FactCheck, the Coventry Observer and Guido Fawkes (who accused her of 'spinning' the pledge), fact-checked by Full Fact, and queried by the Commons Education Committee — making it one of the most-scrutinised domestic-policy claims of the week and the central education flashpoint as overall teacher numbers fell.
entries.explanation: The specific number Phillipson cites is accurate, but it is true only because of how narrowly the target is defined — and it sits alongside an overall fall in teacher numbers that the headline framing obscures. Against the manifesto pledge of at least 6,500 more teachers in secondary schools, special schools/PRUs and further-education colleges by the end of the Parliament (baseline 2023/24), the DfE's 4,654 figure is genuine and is indeed about 72% of the way there. But three caveats matter. First, the target deliberately excludes primary and nursery teachers, and the total full-time-equivalent teaching workforce in state-funded schools actually fell by about 1,900 (from 468,272 to 466,372) between November 2024 and November 2025 — the first contraction of the overall workforce since 2019. Second, it is a net-change measure, not 6,500 newly hired individuals, and most of the progress came in the first year (Nov 2023-Nov 2024); only roughly 670 were added in the latest year. Third, the 4,654 total leans heavily on further-education colleges (1,646 of it), whose inclusion critics say was added to the pledge after the fact; the DfE's own release credits just 3,008 secondary and special-school teachers. So the claim is literally correct on its own terms, which is why Full Fact rates the pledge 'appears on track', but the '70% of the way' line gives a misleadingly rosy impression of teacher supply overall.
evidence: DfE School Workforce in England statistics (published 4 June 2026): 4,654 additional teachers recruited towards the 6,500 target since the 2023/24 baseline — about 72% of the way there.
DfE breakdown: that 4,654 comprises 3,008 secondary and special-school teachers plus 1,646 in further education; the FE element is the contested part of the count.
Total FTE teachers in state-funded schools fell by about 1,900 (468,272 to 466,372) between November 2024 and November 2025 — the first fall in the overall school workforce since 2019.

Full Fact (June 2026): the 4,654 figure and '70%-plus' framing are accurate, but the target excludes primary and nursery teachers; most progress (around 4,000 of the gain) came in the first year, with only roughly 670 added in the latest year.
The pledge is a net-change target ('at least 6,500 more teachers... at the end of this Parliament than at the start'), not 6,500 brand-new hires, and Full Fact rates it 'appears on track'.
Primary teachers fell about 1.3% and secondary teachers about 0.4% year-on-year; special/PRU teachers rose about 3.9% (DfE, 4 June 2026).
entries.confidence: High — the figures come directly from the DfE's 4 June 2026 School Workforce statistics and are corroborated independently by Full Fact and Schools Week, which reach the same conclusion: the number is right but the framing omits the falling overall workforce and the narrow target definition.

sources.title: Full Fact — Do the latest teacher figures show Labour is on track to deliver its manifesto recruitment pledge?
Full Fact — How many of the promised 6,500 'new expert teachers' has the government recruited?
Schools Week — DfE 'hits 70% of 6,500 teachers target', but overall workforce shrinks
GOV.UK — School Workforce in England

entries.claim: We're now in a situation for the first time ever where the amount that we are paying in benefits is more than the government is taking in income tax.
entries.speaker: Katie Lam
entries.role: Conservative MP for Weald of Kent and Opposition Assistant Whip
entries.discussionNote: The line that welfare now exceeds income tax 'for the first time ever' has become one of the most-repeated Conservative and Reform attack lines of the welfare debate, deployed by Katie Lam on BBC Radio 4's World at One and echoed almost word-for-word by Robert Jenrick, Helen Whately, Kemi Badenoch and Luke Evans across broadcast and social media. Full Fact has had to fact-check the framing more than once, and it remains a high-reach talking point as the parties spar over the size of the benefits bill.
entries.explanation: Lam's underlying fact — that total welfare spending currently exceeds income tax receipts — is correct, but the 'for the first time ever' framing is the opposite of the truth. Using OBR figures, Full Fact and the IFS find welfare (which includes the state pension, not just out-of-work benefits) has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years, going back to at least 2013/14. Far from being the first time, 2025/26 is expected to be the LAST year for the foreseeable future that welfare exceeds income tax: the OBR forecasts the gap narrowing sharply and the positions reversing in 2026/27, with income tax staying ahead through to the end of the forecast in 2030/31. The figures also do not cleanly map onto 'workers paying for non-workers' — many workers receive benefits and many non-workers, including pensioners, pay income tax.
evidence: Full Fact (June 2026), citing OBR figures: welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years, going back to at least 2013/14 — not for the first time.

IFS (Tom Waters): welfare spending is forecast to be lower than income tax receipts in 2026/27 'for the first time since at least 2013/14', and to remain that way until the end of the forecast period in 2030/31.
The gap between welfare and income tax narrowed significantly in 2025/26, so 2025/26 is expected to be the last year — not the first — that welfare exceeds income tax.

Welfare here includes the state pension alongside universal credit and other benefits, so the totals are not a clean measure of money paid by workers versus money spent on non-workers; many workers claim benefits and many pensioners pay income tax.
The same 'first time ever' framing was used by other senior figures including Robert Jenrick, Helen Whately, Kemi Badenoch and Luke Evans, and was rated false in each case.
entries.confidence: High — corroborated by two Full Fact analyses and the IFS, all drawing on OBR public-finances data; the OBR is the underlying primary source.

sources.title: Full Fact — Katie Lam wrongly claims welfare has exceeded income tax 'for the first time ever'
Full Fact — Welfare payments have not exceeded income tax 'for the first time ever'
OBR — Economic and fiscal outlook (March 2026)

entries.claim: We'd stop the boats — we'd stop 1,000 people coming in a day.
entries.speaker: Robert Kenyon
entries.role: Reform UK candidate, Makerfield by-election (18 June 2026)
entries.discussionNote: Immigration remains the single most-cited issue facing Britain, and this claim landed on BBC Question Time (4 June) in the run-up to the high-profile Makerfield by-election on 18 June, where Reform UK's Robert Kenyon faces Labour's Andy Burnham. The contest is one of the most-watched political events of the month. Kenyon's '1,000 people a day' line was clipped and shared by LBC, fact-checked by Full Fact, and drew sustained social-media reaction, keeping it near the top of the week's immigration debate.
entries.explanation: Kenyon's framing — that stopping the boats would stop '1,000 people coming in a day' — presents 1,000 daily arrivals as the normal current rate. That overstates the real figure by roughly ten times. Home Office data show that since Labour took office on 5 July 2024, small-boat arrivals have averaged about 106 people per day overall (around 274 per day if days with zero crossings are excluded). Days of 1,000 or more arrivals are rare: there have been just 12 such days in the entire dataset since 2018, and only four since Labour took office. The Migration Observatory puts total crossings at roughly 41,000 in both 2024 and 2025 (about 112 a day), with 2026 running sharply lower — around 9,000 in the first five months. There is a grain of truth that 1,000-arrival days do occasionally happen in peak summer conditions, but presenting that as the everyday rate is a significant exaggeration.
evidence: Full Fact (June 2026): small-boat arrivals have averaged about 106 per day since Labour took office on 5 July 2024 (274 per day excluding days with no crossings) — not 1,000.
Full Fact: only 12 days since the dataset began on 1 January 2018 have seen 1,000+ small-boat arrivals; just four of those fall since July 2024 (31 May 2025; 6 and 19 September 2025; 8 October 2025).
Migration Observatory: roughly 41,000 crossings in 2024 and about 41,000 in 2025 — an average of around 112 people per day across the year.
Migration Observatory: about 9,000 people crossed in the first five months of 2026, around 38% lower than the same period in 2025, so the current daily rate is well below even the long-run average.
The highest annual total to date was about 46,000 in 2022; no year has come close to the ~365,000 that a sustained rate of 1,000 a day would imply.
entries.confidence: High — corroborated by Full Fact's analysis of Home Office small-boat arrivals data and the Migration Observatory's Channel crossings briefing, two independent sources.

sources.title: Full Fact — How many migrants come to the UK by small boat each day?
Migration Observatory — People crossing the English Channel in small boats
ITV News Granada — Reform UK and Andy Burnham to go head to head in Makerfield by-election

entries.topic: UK economic growth vs the G7
entries.claim: Britain is the fastest growing economy in the G7 — that is proof our economic plan is working.
entries.speaker: Rachel Reeves
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer fighting for his job amid a Labour leadership crisis, Reeves leaned hard on the Q1 2026 GDP figures (released 14 May) as the government's central defence, repeating the 'fastest growing economy in the G7' line in interviews and party briefings through late May and into early June. The boast was contested by Conservative and Reform commentators and picked apart by independent statisticians, making it one of the most-argued economic claims of the week and a recurring flashpoint in coverage of Labour's record.
entries.explanation: Taken narrowly, the claim is accurate: ONS data show UK real GDP grew 0.6% in Q1 2026 (January to March), the strongest of any G7 economy that quarter — ahead of the United States (0.5%), Canada (0.4%), Germany (0.3%), Italy (0.2%) and France (0.0%). Real GDP per head also rose 0.6%, the fastest in four years. But the unqualified phrasing implies sustained leadership, and that does not hold. In Q4 2025 the UK was in the lower half of the G7 (0.2% growth), and across the whole of 2025 its 1.4% annual growth trailed the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%). The IMF's January 2026 forecast puts the UK third in the G7 for 2026, behind the US and Canada. So the UK led the G7 for a single quarter, not on a sustained basis — and Labour's own manifesto pledge was for 'the highest sustained growth in the G7', which Full Fact rates as 'appears off track'.
evidence: ONS GDP first quarterly estimate (released 14 May 2026), Table 5: UK Q1 2026 quarterly growth 0.6% — ahead of the US (0.5%), Canada (0.4%), Germany (0.3%), Italy (0.2%) and France (0.0%).
ONS: UK real GDP per head rose 0.6% in Q1 2026 (0.9% year-on-year), the fastest quarterly per-head growth in about four years; Q4 2025 growth was revised to 0.2%.
ONS Table 5: for full-year 2025 the UK grew 1.4%, behind the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%) though ahead of the European G7 members.
IMF World Economic Outlook update (January 2026): the UK is forecast to be the third fastest-growing G7 economy in 2026 (1.3%) and 2027 (1.5%), behind the US and Canada.

Full Fact government tracker: Labour's pledge of 'the highest sustained growth in the G7' is rated 'appears off track' — the UK has led on GDP per head in only one quarter (Q1 2025) since July 2024, not consistently.
entries.confidence: High — the single-quarter ranking is confirmed by the ONS first quarterly estimate (with its own G7 comparison table) and corroborated by OECD Q1 2026 data, while the sustained-growth caveat rests on IMF projections and Full Fact's tracker.

entries.claim: Mild anxiety is not a reason to be on disability benefit — it just isn't. We can't afford it, it can't continue.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader, Reform UK; MP for Clacton
entries.discussionNote: Farage's welfare reform push has dominated the week alongside the Nowak/two-tier policing row. This specific claim — that 'mild anxiety' should not qualify for disability benefits — was posted on his official Facebook and repeated in ITV and GB News interviews, drawing strong reactions from disability rights groups and Labour. Reform's pledge to cut £40bn from the welfare bill makes the factual basis of this claim central to the political debate.
entries.explanation: There is a genuine and well-documented rise in disability benefit claims for mental health conditions, particularly among young people — the number of 16-to-24-year-olds awarded PIP per month has tripled, and mental health conditions now account for over 35% of PIP claims, up from about 25% a decade ago. In that sense Farage is pointing at a real trend. However, 'mild anxiety' is a misleading characterisation of who receives PIP: the assessment process requires claimants to demonstrate significant functional limitations in daily living or mobility, and the vast majority of successful mental health claimants have moderate-to-severe conditions, often with comorbidities. PIP is not awarded for a diagnosis alone but for its impact on daily life. The framing implies the system hands out benefits for trivial conditions, which misrepresents how PIP assessments work.
evidence: DWP PIP statistics (January 2026): 3.9 million people claiming PIP in England and Wales, with psychiatric disorders the most common condition category (39% of all PIP claimants).
Centre for Social Justice analysis: 633,000 people were claiming PIP for anxiety and mood disorders as of July 2025, with almost 250 new awards per day in this category.
The number of 16-to-24-year-olds receiving PIP per month has tripled from 2,967 to 7,857.
IFS research: more than half the rise in 16-to-64-year-olds claiming disability benefits since the pandemic is due to mental health or behavioural conditions.

PIP eligibility is based on functional impact, not diagnosis: claimants must score points across daily living and mobility activities, meaning 'mild' cases that do not significantly affect functioning would not typically qualify.
ONS data: almost one million 16-to-24-year-olds are neither in work nor training, with over half citing ill-health.
entries.confidence: High — DWP PIP statistics, Centre for Social Justice analysis and IFS research all corroborate the underlying trend; PIP eligibility criteria are published by DWP.

sources.title: DWP — Personal Independence Payment statistics (January 2026)
Centre for Social Justice — Disability benefits for anxiety hit new record high
IFS — The role of changing health in rising health-related benefit claims
Disability News Service — Farage finally confirms: A Reform UK government would slash spending on disability benefits

entries.claim: We have identified up to £40 billion in potential savings through tighter eligibility checks, reductions in fraud and administrative reform across the welfare system.
entries.speaker: Robert Jenrick
entries.discussionNote: Reform UK's welfare reform pitch intensified this week as Farage declared a 'war on benefits'. Jenrick's £40bn savings figure — used to justify both sweeping welfare cuts and the party's commitment to the pensions triple lock — was widely reported and challenged. The US State Department's intervention on 'two-tier policing' kept Reform in the headlines, adding to the amplification of the party's domestic policy proposals.
entries.explanation: The £40bn figure is not supported by any independent costing. For context, the Labour government's own welfare reforms — described as the most ambitious in a generation — were projected by the OBR to save just £1.9bn by 2030/31, and the IFS noted that even those savings were reduced by £3bn after the government's U-turn on disability benefit cuts. The Conservative Party's 2024 manifesto claimed £12bn in welfare savings, and the Resolution Foundation called even that 'extremely challenging to implement'. Reform has published no detailed breakdown of how £40bn would be achieved, and the party's own policy documents list broad aspirations (tighter checks, fraud reduction, restricting benefits to UK citizens) without costings. Independent welfare analysts have not validated this figure.
evidence: Reform UK claims £40bn in annual welfare savings from tighter eligibility, fraud reduction and administrative reform — no independent costing published.
OBR scored the Labour government's welfare reforms at £1.9bn savings by 2030/31 (Gov.uk, March 2026).
IFS: changes to the government's benefit reforms reduced projected savings by £3bn in 2029/30.
The Conservative Party's 2024 manifesto claimed £12bn in welfare savings; the Resolution Foundation called this 'extremely challenging to implement'.
Total welfare spending in 2025/26 was approximately £333bn (OBR). A £40bn cut would represent a 12% reduction — a scale unprecedented in modern UK welfare history.
Reform's policy page lists broad headings (restrict welfare to UK nationals, tighter PIP assessments) but provides no line-by-line costings.
entries.confidence: High — OBR, IFS and Resolution Foundation analyses provide clear benchmarks against which the £40bn claim can be assessed. No independent body has endorsed the figure.

sources.title: Gov.uk — Reforms to welfare system set to save £1.9 billion by 2030/31
IFS — Changes to benefit reforms reduce saving by £3bn in 2029/30
British Brief — Reform UK reverses stance on triple lock, pledges £40bn welfare cuts to fund pension guarantee
London Loves Business — Farage unveils sweeping welfare cuts in bid to slash £330bn benefits bill

entries.topic: Welfare spending vs income tax revenue
entries.claim: The UK has crossed a line. Welfare spending: £333 billion. Income tax revenue: £331 billion. We're now spending more on welfare than we raise in income tax. This is a first in our history.
entries.speaker: Luke Evans
entries.discussionNote: The claim that welfare spending has overtaken income tax 'for the first time ever' went viral across social media in early June 2026, shared by Conservative and Reform UK politicians, UKIP, and commentators. Full Fact published a detailed rebuttal on June 4, making it one of the most fact-checked claims of the week. It was amplified alongside the broader debate about Reform UK's welfare reform agenda.
entries.explanation: The headline figures are broadly correct: OBR data for 2025/26 shows welfare spending of approximately £333bn against income tax receipts of approximately £331bn. However, the claim that this is 'a first in our history' is wrong. Full Fact found that welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts in every year since at least 2013/14 — making this at least the thirteenth consecutive year, not a watershed moment. Furthermore, the OBR forecasts that income tax receipts will exceed welfare spending again in 2026/27. The misleading framing — presenting a longstanding pattern as a sudden crisis — is what makes the claim mostly false despite the underlying numbers being accurate.
evidence: OBR Economic and Fiscal Outlook (March 2026): welfare spending £333bn, income tax receipts £331bn in 2025/26.
Full Fact (4 June 2026): welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year since at least 2013/14. The 'first time ever' framing is false.
OBR forecasts income tax receipts will overtake welfare spending in 2026/27 for the first time since at least 2013/14.
Welfare spending includes the state pension (approximately £130bn), which is the single largest component — not just working-age benefits.
The claim was repeated by UKIP, multiple Conservative MPs and Reform UK figures, all using the 'first time' framing that Full Fact debunked.
entries.confidence: High — figures verified directly against OBR data; Full Fact's analysis provides definitive context on the historical pattern.

sources.title: Full Fact — Welfare payments have not exceeded income tax 'for the first time ever'
OBR — Economic and Fiscal Outlook, March 2026
Dr Luke Evans MP — Labour spends more on welfare than it raises in Income Tax
VisaVerge — UK Welfare vs Income Tax: Full Fact 2026 Analysis

entries.claim: It is now clear to growing millions in this country that we are living under two-tier policing.
entries.speaker: Nigel Farage
entries.role: Leader, Reform UK; MP for Clacton
entries.discussionNote: Farage's 'two-tier policing' claim at PMQs on 3 June, delivered in the wake of the Henry Nowak murder conviction and the Southampton riots, was the single most discussed political moment of the week. It provoked direct rebukes from the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition, Baroness Lawrence, the London Mayor, and later the US State Department. It trended on social media, dominated front pages, and triggered a parliamentary row.
entries.explanation: Farage's claim presents an assertion of systemic, deliberate bias in policing as established fact. The available evidence does not support this. The Home Affairs Committee's report on the police response to the Southport riots found no evidence of two-tier policing. The Independent Office for Police Conduct is investigating the specific conduct of officers in the Nowak case, but one tragic case of apparent individual failure does not demonstrate a systemic policy of differential treatment by ethnicity. Moreover, the statistical evidence on policing disparities points in the opposite direction to Farage's implication: Home Office stop-and-search data for the year ending March 2025 shows Black people were stopped at 3.8 times the rate of White people, and Asian people at a higher rate than White people — the reverse of the anti-white bias Farage alleges. The Nowak case raises serious questions about the officers involved, but it is not evidence of a nationwide two-tier system.
evidence: Home Affairs Committee report on police response to Southport riots: found no evidence of two-tier policing in officers' handling of violence and criminality.

Home Office stop-and-search statistics (year ending March 2025): Black people stopped at 3.8 times the rate of White people; 53,949 searches of Black individuals vs 287,307 of White individuals despite Black people comprising a far smaller share of the population.
IOPC opened an investigation into the conduct of officers who handcuffed Henry Nowak — an investigation into individual officers, not a finding of systemic bias.
Henry Nowak's father Mark Nowak told reporters: 'We do not want his death to be used to create further division, hatred or tension.'
The claim was rejected by the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition (Kemi Badenoch), Baroness Lawrence, and the Deputy Prime Minister (David Lammy).
entries.confidence: High — Home Affairs Committee findings, Home Office stop-and-search statistics and IOPC statements all directly relevant. No official body or independent review has found evidence of systemic two-tier policing as Farage describes.

sources.title: Home Office — Stop and search, arrests and mental health detentions, year ending March 2025
LBC — Farage doubles down on 'two-tier policing' claims as Khan, Lawrence and Starmer round on him
LSE British Politics and Policy — The truth about 'two-tier policing'
NPR — In Britain, video of a dying teen handcuffed by police sparks outrage and riots

entries.topic: UK economic growth vs the G7
entries.claim: We now have the fastest-growing economy in the G7, net migration has fallen and NHS waiting lists are at their lowest level in three-and-a-half years.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.discussionNote: Starmer is repeating this line — delivered to reporters outside Downing Street — as the centrepiece of his defence of his record amid a Labour leadership crisis (around 30 MPs pressing for a timetable to go, and a 18 June Greater Manchester by-election that could trigger a contest). With his approval ratings at a 50-year low for a PM at this stage, the 'fastest-growing economy in the G7' boast is the most-scrutinised factual claim in UK politics this week, amplified by Bloomberg, GB News and broadsheet coverage.
entries.explanation: On the latest single quarter the claim holds: ONS figures (released 14 May 2026) show UK GDP grew 0.6% in Q1 2026 (Jan-Mar), the highest of the G7 countries that had reported — ahead of the US (0.5%), Germany (0.3%), Italy (0.2%) and France (0.0%). But it needs three caveats. Japan had not yet published comparable Q1 2026 data when the claim was made, so 'fastest in the G7' was not fully confirmable. It is one quarter, not a trend: Full Fact's tracker rates the government's 'highest sustained growth in the G7' pledge as 'off track', noting the UK had joint second-lowest G7 growth in the final quarter of 2025. And there is a recognised pattern of strong UK first quarters that peter out (seen in 2024 and 2025). On full-year 2025 growth (1.4%) the UK trailed both the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%).
evidence: ONS GDP first quarterly estimate (released 14 May 2026): UK real GDP +0.6% in Q1 2026, following revised +0.2% in Q4 2025.
ONS G7 comparison in the same bulletin: UK 0.6%, US 0.5%, Germany 0.3%, Italy 0.2%, France 0.0% — UK highest of those reporting.
Japan had not yet released comparable Q1 2026 figures, so the full G7 ranking was incomplete when the claim was made (Full Fact tracker, updated 19 May 2026).

Full Fact rates the manifesto pledge of the 'highest sustained growth in the G7' as 'appears off track': the UK had joint second-lowest G7 growth in Q4 2025 and did not lead on real GDP per head in two of the three preceding quarters.
Full-year 2025 growth: UK 1.4%, behind the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%), ahead of France, Germany and Italy.
Commentators note a pattern of strong UK Q1 growth that fades later in the year (2024 and 2025), so a single quarter overstates the durability of the lead.
entries.confidence: High — Q1 2026 figures taken directly from the ONS quarterly GDP bulletin and corroborated by Full Fact's government tracker and House of Commons Library GDP comparisons.

sources.title: ONS — GDP first quarterly estimate, UK: January to March 2026 (released 14 May 2026)
Full Fact — Is the government on course to secure 'the highest sustained growth in the G7'?
The London Economic — UK expected to have fastest economic growth of G7 nations so far in 2026 (14 May 2026)
House of Commons Library — GDP international comparisons: Economic indicators
Bloomberg — Britain's Economy Turns Sour Just as Starmer Had a Reason to Brag (22 May 2026)

entries.topic: Net migration since the Conservatives left office
entries.claim: Net migration is now at 171,000, down from a high of 944,000 under the Conservatives.
entries.speaker: Shabana Mahmood
entries.discussionNote: Immigration is the single most-cited issue facing Britain in the May 2026 Ipsos Issues Index (41%). The ONS released the year-ending-December-2025 net migration figure on 21 May 2026, and the government's framing of the fall remains the dominant political talking point into early June, repeated by the Home Secretary and contested by Reform UK.
entries.explanation: Both numbers match the official ONS estimates. Provisional net migration for the year ending December 2025 was 171,000, and the revised peak under the Conservatives was 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 — an 82% fall, exactly as stated. The only caveat is that the 171,000 figure is provisional and the ONS has a track record of revising recent net-migration estimates upwards as more complete data arrive, so the precise figure may rise somewhat.
evidence: ONS Long-term international migration, provisional (released 21 May 2026): net migration 171,000 for year ending December 2025, down from a revised 331,000 for year ending December 2024.
ONS revised the peak to 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 (up from a previous estimate of 906,000 in year ending June 2023) — the high point reached under the Conservative government.
171,000 vs 944,000 is a fall of roughly 82%, consistent with the Home Secretary's accompanying claim of 'down 82%'.
Main driver: non-EU+ work-related arrivals fell about 47% in 2025; the YE Dec 2025 level is the lowest since early 2021.
Caveat: the 171,000 figure is provisional; the ONS has repeatedly revised recent estimates upward (e.g. YE June 2023 was revised up by 166,000), so the final figure may be higher.
entries.confidence: High — figures taken directly from ONS provisional migration statistics and corroborated by the Migration Observatory and House of Commons Library.

sources.title: ONS — Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025 (21 May 2026)
ONS blog — Net migration continues to fall (21 May 2026)
Migration Observatory — Net migration to the UK
Shabana Mahmood (X) — 'Net migration is now at 171,000, down from a high of 944,000 under the Conservatives'

entries.topic: Cost of housing asylum seekers in hotels
entries.claim: Housing asylum seekers in hotels costs the UK taxpayer around £8 million a day.
entries.speaker: Rishi Sunak
entries.role: Then Prime Minister; figure still widely repeated across the right in 2026 immigration debate
entries.discussionNote: Immigration is the single most-cited issue facing Britain in the May 2026 Ipsos Issues Index (41%, up 9 points), and the '£8 million a day' figure remains the most repeated number in that debate.
entries.explanation: The figure was accurate at its peak but is out of date as a present-tense claim. At the height of hotel use (June 2023) about 51,000 asylum seekers were in 400+ hotels, and in 2023/24 the Home Office spent roughly £3bn on hotels — about £8.3m a day. But by 2024/25 hotel spend had fallen to about £2.1bn, or roughly £5.77m a day (down ~17%). So 'around £8 million a day' was true two years ago, not for the latest year.
evidence: Home Office 2023/24 accounts: ~£4.7bn on asylum support, of which ~£3bn on hotels ≈ £8.3m/day.
Peak June 2023: ~51,000 asylum seekers in 400+ hotels.
Home Office 2024/25: ~£2.1bn on hotels ≈ £5.77m/day, a ~17% fall year-on-year.
Average hotel cost ~£170 per person per night in 2024/25 vs ~£27 for non-hotel accommodation; nightly rates fell from £162 (Apr 2024) to £119 (Mar 2025).
Hotels were 76% of asylum accommodation cost but housed only ~35% of supported people in 2024/25.
NAO: the asylum accommodation contracts, originally estimated at £4.5bn, are now projected to cost £15.3bn over ten years.
entries.confidence: High — corroborated across the National Audit Office, the Migration Observatory and Full Fact.

sources.title: Full Fact — Housing asylum seekers in hotels costs around £8 million a day

NAO — The Home Office's management of asylum accommodation contracts (HC 874, 7 May 2025)
NAO — An analysis of the asylum system (10 Dec 2025)
Ipsos — Immigration seen as the biggest issue facing Britain (May 2026)

entries.claim: There are more people in work than there were before the pandemic.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.explanation: The claim counts only employees, not the self-employed. Including the self-employed, total employment is around 600,000 lower than before the pandemic, so the headline framing is misleading.
evidence: The 'more people in work' figure relies on the employee count only.
Total employment (employees plus self-employed) is roughly 600,000 below the pre-pandemic level.
entries.confidence: Medium-High — per Full Fact; reconfirm latest ONS Labour Market figures.

sources.title: Full Fact — Prime Minister's Questions fact-checked

entries.claim: Disability benefit spending could exceed defence spending by the end of the decade.
entries.speaker: Tony Blair
entries.explanation: The direction is correct but the timeframe understates reality: spending on these benefits has, on some measures, already exceeded defence spending rather than waiting until the end of the decade.
evidence: Full Fact found the threshold the claim projects for the future has, on some measures, already been crossed.
entries.confidence: Medium — per Full Fact (28 May 2026); reconfirm exact spending definitions.

sources.title: Full Fact — Latest fact checks

entries.claim: Scrapping net zero would save tens of billions and help fund roughly £70bn a year of tax cuts.
entries.speaker: Richard Tice
entries.explanation: Independent analysts dispute that abandoning net zero produces savings on this scale, and the wider £70bn tax-cut costing is contested. Several net-zero programmes are projected to create jobs and investment rather than only impose costs.
evidence: Reform's manifesto proposes ~£70bn/year in tax cuts, partly funded by cancelling net-zero goals — a costing contested by independent analysts.
Government Clean Energy Jobs Plan projects 800,000 clean-energy jobs by 2030; TUC Cymru warned Reform's stance risks ~40,000 manufacturing jobs in Wales.
entries.confidence: Medium — claim direction well documented; savings figures contested. Reconfirm against costings.

entries.topic: Size of the NHS waiting list
entries.claim: Around 7 million people are on the NHS England waiting list.
entries.speaker: Wes Streeting
entries.explanation: This conflates cases with people. There are about 7.4 million cases (treatments) on the list but only about 6.3 million people, because many are waiting for more than one treatment. Quoting ~7 million people overstates the number of individuals affected.
evidence: NHS England (Jan 2025): ~6.3 million people on the list, ~7.4 million cases/treatments.
Full Fact flagged the health secretary repeatedly overstating the number of people.
entries.confidence: High — per Full Fact and NHS England statistics.

sources.title: Full Fact — Health secretary again overstates size of NHS waiting list
